summary: Matt Rocklin announces a new weekly development log series to increase transparency around Dask development, which has accelerated due to increased adoption and corporate funding. The post covers four main development areas: embedded Bokeh servers for worker diagnostics, a redesigned scheduler architecture that shifts intelligence to workers, new dataframe algorithms, and the fastparquet library. The scheduler changes move responsibility from a centralized scheduler to smarter workers, enabling better resource utilization and features like GPU constraints. These architectural changes are expected to yield around 30% speedup for dataframe operations while also simplifying the codebase for contributors.
key_points: Embedded Bokeh servers in workers now enable rapid diagnostic visualization development - creating a new diagnostic is faster than grepping through logs
The scheduler architecture has been inverted: workers are now smarter and receive all ready tasks immediately rather than being fed tasks just-in-time
Moving logic from scheduler to workers paradoxically made both components easier to reason about, lowering barriers for contributors
The changes enable resource constraints (GPU, RAM requirements) that the simpler workers couldn't handle
Fastparquet provides a Pythonic Parquet reader/writer using Numba with good performance
money_quotes: It is now faster for me to build a new diagnostic than to grep through logs.
Previously the scheduler knew everything and the workers were fairly simple-minded.
This is better locally but slightly worse globally.
By moving logic from the scheduler to the worker we've actually moved them both easier to reason about.
Increased activity is great, however an unintended side effect is that I have spent less time writing about development and engaging with the broader community.
Everyone running a Tornado application should have an embedded Bokeh server running.
key_insight: Distributing intelligence from a centralized scheduler to smarter workers can improve both performance and code simplicity, even though it trades global optimization for local efficiency.
